items.q: What process moves carbon from the atmosphere into plants?
items.why: Students confuse the directions. Photosynthesis takes CO2 OUT of the atmosphere (plants build sugars); respiration RELEASES CO2. Both run simultaneously in plants, but only photosynthesis fixes new carbon.

items.q: Why is burning fossil fuels considered to disrupt the carbon cycle?
items.correct: It releases carbon stored for millions of years much faster than the cycle can absorb.
items.misconception: It creates new carbon that did not exist before.
items.why: Students think combustion creates carbon. It RELEASES previously stored carbon - the issue is rate. Geological storage took millions of years; we're releasing it in centuries, overwhelming natural sinks.

items.q: What does the 'tragedy of the commons' describe?
items.correct: Shared resources tend to be overexploited because individuals benefit personally while sharing costs.
items.misconception: It is a play about poor people.
items.why: Students take 'commons' as a place. Hardin's metaphor describes a STRUCTURAL PROBLEM: rational individual choices lead to collective ruin when costs are externalised - applies to overfishing, climate, and groundwater.
The answer examiners want is: Scope 2. “Scope 1” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Global warming potential (GWP). “Radiative forcing units” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If a company emits 200 t Scope 1, 500 t Scope 2 and 2300 t Scope 3, total CO₂e (t) is?
items.why: The real figure is 3000. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
